aliases.name: Gorgo / Gorgon
aliases.meaning: 'Dreadful one'; used for all three Gorgon sisters

description.summary: In Greek mythology, the only mortal of the three Gorgon sisters, whose gaze turned onlookers to stone and who was beheaded by the hero Perseus; her severed head, the Gorgoneion, became Athena's apotropaic symbol set on the Aegis.
description.full: Medusa (Ancient Greek: Μέδουσα, Médousa) is a figure of Greek mythology, one of the three Gorgon sisters — Stheno, Euryale and Medusa — daughters of the primordial sea-deities Phorcys and Ceto. Of the three only Medusa was mortal. She is described from Hesiod onward as a winged, snake-haired creature whose direct gaze petrified the viewer. The hero Perseus, sent by King Polydectes of Seriphos to fetch her head, used a polished shield from Athena, winged sandals from Hermes, an adamantine sickle and the cap of invisibility of Hades to behead her without meeting her eyes. From her severed neck sprang the winged horse Pegasus and the warrior Chrysaor, fathered by Poseidon. Perseus carried the head as a weapon and finally gave it to Athena, who set it (the Gorgoneion) on her shield, the Aegis. Ovid's Metamorphoses (Roman, 1st century CE) gives an influential later version in which Medusa was originally a beautiful mortal raped by Poseidon in Athena's temple and punished by Athena with her serpentine transformation — the version on which most modern feminist readings of Medusa as victim-turned-symbol draw.

etymology.origin: Ancient Greek Μέδουσα (Médousa), feminine present participle of the verb μέδω (medō, 'to rule, to guard, to protect').
etymology.first_use: Earliest extant attestations in Hesiod's Theogony (c. 8th century BCE) and Homer (Iliad, Odyssey)

related_phenomena.name: Gorgons (Stheno, Euryale, Medusa)
related_phenomena.description: The three-sister Gorgon class of which Medusa is the mortal member

related_phenomena.name: Siren
related_phenomena.description: Other female Greek mythological figures of dangerous beauty

related_phenomena.name: Basilisk
related_phenomena.description: Later Mediterranean creature whose gaze likewise kills

features.description: Living serpents in place of hair, sometimes also serpents girding her body
Direct eye-contact with Medusa turns the viewer to stone
Golden wings on her back in Hesiod and most archaic depictions
Archaic depictions show large protruding tusks and a wide grimace
Sometimes attributed in archaic and classical accounts
Her severed head retains its petrifying power and is set on the Aegis of Athena and on Perseus's shield

physical.morphology: Hybrid female monster — winged, serpent-haired, with bestial features in archaic art (tusks, protruding tongue) softened in classical and later art into a beautiful but terrifying woman.

abilities.description: Anyone meeting Medusa's eyes is turned to stone
Her severed head retains its petrifying power and is wielded as a weapon by Perseus and as an apotropaic talisman by Athena
Pegasus and Chrysaor spring from her neck at her death; the blood from her left side is poison and from her right side a healing balm (Apollodorus)

habitat_preference: Edge of the world (the far west, beyond Ocean)
Cave or rocky outcrop in later sources
patterns: Lives at the world's edge with her two immortal sisters
Sleeps when Perseus arrives, allowing her killing

range.description: Medusa is a figure of ancient Greek mythology, transmitted across the Greek and Roman Mediterranean and into the broader European tradition through Latin sources (notably Ovid).
regions: Mythic 'far west' beyond Ocean (in archaic geography)

hotspots.name: Seriphos / Argolid

location.description: Mythic settings of the Perseus cycle: Seriphos (where Polydectes ruled) and Argos (Perseus's home)

hotspots.notes: Primary mythic settings of the Perseus–Medusa narrative

hotspots.name: Temple of Artemis, Corfu

location.description: Pediment of the Temple of Artemis at Corfu (c. 580 BCE) bearing one of the most famous archaic Gorgon depictions

hotspots.notes: Archaeological example of archaic Gorgoneion iconography

first_recorded.source: Hesiod, Theogony; Homer, Iliad and Odyssey

peak_activity_periods.period: Archaic and Classical Greece (c. 8th–4th c. BCE)
peak_activity_periods.description: Establishment of the Gorgoneion as apotropaic motif and of the Perseus cycle as canonical narrative

peak_activity_periods.period: Roman period (1st c. BCE – 1st c. CE)
peak_activity_periods.description: Ovid's Metamorphoses introduces the Athena-curse / rape variant that dominates later reception
Reuse in psychoanalysis (Freud), feminist criticism (Cixous), and popular culture

origins.cultural_roots: Medusa belongs to the archaic Greek pantheon of chthonic, female monsters of the primordial generations preceding the Olympians; her snake hair and petrifying gaze reflect a wider Mediterranean apotropaic tradition.
origins.earliest_accounts: Hesiod (Theogony) and Homer (Iliad, Odyssey), 8th century BCE.
origins.folklore_connections: Connected with other gaze-creatures (basilisk, cockatrice) in later European tradition, and with broader Mediterranean apotropaic eye motifs.

timeline.event: Hesiod's Theogony names the three Gorgons (Sthenno, Euryale, Medousa) as daughters of Phorcys and Ceto and recounts Medusa's beheading and the birth of Pegasus and Chrysaor
Pediment of the Temple of Artemis, Corfu, executed with a colossal Gorgon Medusa
Ovid's Metamorphoses (Book IV) gives the Athena/Poseidon-temple version of Medusa's transformation
timeline.significance: Foundational text for the later 'Medusa as victim' reading

timeline.event: Benvenuto Cellini completes his bronze Perseus with the Head of Medusa for the Loggia dei Lanzi, Florence
timeline.significance: Definitive Renaissance treatment of the myth

timeline.event: Caravaggio paints his shield-like Medusa for Cardinal Francesco Maria del Monte
timeline.significance: Among the most famous early-modern depictions

timeline.event: Sigmund Freud writes 'Das Medusenhaupt' (published posthumously 1940), reading Medusa as a symbol of castration anxiety
Hélène Cixous publishes 'Le Rire de la Méduse' (The Laugh of the Medusa)
timeline.significance: Foundational feminist reading reclaiming Medusa as a symbol of female creative power

history.modern_era: In the 20th and 21st centuries Medusa has been reread by psychoanalysts (Freud), feminists (Cixous, Bowers), and post-#MeToo writers as a victim-turned-symbol of female agency, and has remained omnipresent in fashion (Versace), film (Clash of the Titans, 1981 and 2010), video games and tattoo culture.

documentary.name: Hesiod, Theogony 270–283
documentary.description: Earliest surviving canonical text naming the three Gorgons and describing the beheading of Medusa.
documentary.analysis: Foundational source for the entire later Medusa tradition.

documentary.name: Pseudo-Apollodorus, Bibliotheca II
documentary.description: Detailed prose narrative of the Perseus cycle, including the Graeae, the cap of Hades, and the beheading of Medusa.
documentary.analysis: Standard mythographic reference for the full Perseus narrative.

documentary.name: Ovid, Metamorphoses IV.770–803
documentary.description: Ovid's account in which Medusa is originally beautiful and is transformed by Athena after Poseidon's rape in Athena's temple.
documentary.analysis: Foundational source for the later 'Medusa as victim' tradition.

documentary.name: Perseus with the Head of Medusa (Cellini, 1554)
documentary.description: Bronze sculpture by Benvenuto Cellini in the Loggia dei Lanzi, Florence.
documentary.analysis: Definitive Renaissance treatment of the myth.

scientific.name: Apotropaic-image origin
scientific.description: Medusa originated as the personification of the apotropaic Gorgoneion, a wide-grinning protective face placed on shields, temples and amphorae across the archaic Greek and broader Mediterranean world. The narrative of Perseus accreted around the prior cult image.
evidence_cited: Earliest visual Gorgoneia precede coherent narrative attestations
Continuity with Near Eastern apotropaic faces

scientific.name: Psychoanalytic reading
scientific.description: Freud's 1922 essay 'Das Medusenhaupt' reads the snake-haired head as a symbol of castration anxiety; the petrifying effect represents the spectator's response to a perceived loss.
evidence_cited: Symbolic reading of the snake hair and severed head

scientific.name: Feminist reclamation
scientific.description: From Cixous (1975) onward, Medusa is reread as a victim of patriarchal violence (Ovid's account) and as a symbol of suppressed female anger and creative power; this reading has dominated late 20th- and early 21st-century reception.

traditions: Greek mythological tradition (Hesiod, Homer, Pindar, Aeschylus, Pseudo-Apollodorus)
stories: The Gorgoneion as apotropaic device on Athena's Aegis
folklore.beliefs: In archaic and classical Greek practice the image of Medusa (the Gorgoneion) functioned as a powerful apotropaic device, set on shields, temple pediments, and household items to ward off evil.

literature: Hélène Cixous, 'The Laugh of the Medusa' (1975)
Stephen R. Wilk, Medusa: Solving the Mystery of the Gorgon (2000)
film_tv: Clash of the Titans (1981) — stop-motion Medusa by Ray Harryhausen
Percy Jackson & the Olympians: The Lightning Thief (2010)
games: Hades (Supergiant Games, 2020) — Megaera and the Furies
music: 'Medusa' — multiple songs in popular and metal traditions
other: Flag of Sicily (Trinacria with central Gorgoneion)
Cellini's Perseus with the Head of Medusa (1554), Loggia dei Lanzi, Florence
Luciano Garbati's Medusa with the Head of Perseus (2008/2020)

researchers.name: Jane Ellen Harrison
researchers.contribution: Influential reading of the Gorgoneion as a primary apotropaic cult image preceding the Perseus narrative

researchers.name: Stephen R. Wilk
researchers.contribution: Medusa: Solving the Mystery of the Gorgon (2000)

researchers.name: Hélène Cixous
researchers.contribution: Foundational feminist re-reading 'Le Rire de la Méduse' (1975)

research.ongoing_studies: Ongoing scholarship in classics, art history, gender studies, and reception studies; major museum holdings of Gorgoneion iconography are catalogued at the British Museum, the Metropolitan Museum of Art, and the National Archaeological Museum of Athens.

sources.title: Theogony
sources.publication: Loeb Classical Library / Oxford World's Classics

sources.title: Medusa: Solving the Mystery of the Gorgon
Le Rire de la Méduse (The Laugh of the Medusa)
